| **Tear** | Lachrymal Glands | Moderate (via eye-wearable devices) | High – due to direct diffusion of analytes from blood | Low – streamlined detection of proteins, electrolytes, and metabolites | High precision and specificity, similar composition to blood | Limited volume, collection can be challenging without irritation |
| **Sweat** | Eccrine and apocrine sweat glands (via skin) | High – readily available across the body surface | Moderate – concentrations can vary based on activity and hydration | Moderate – influenced by sweat rate, evaporation, and contamination | Non-invasive, widely accessible, suitable for continuous monitoring | Biomarker concentration variability, potential environmental interferences |
| **ISF** | Between skin cells and capillaries | Moderate – accessed via microneedles or skin-permeable patches | High – closely reflects blood analyte levels | Moderate to High – requires minimally invasive access tools | High biomarker relevance; stable composition | Requires specialized access methods; slower sample replenishment |
| **Epidermal** | Outer skin layers | Low – small quantities | Low to Moderate | High – complex matrix and low volume | Potential for superficial, non-invasive sampling | Low fluid volume; difficult to isolate specific analytes |
| **Breath** | Lungs (exhaled air) | High – easy collection | Low to Moderate – depends on target gases (e.g., VOCs) | High – complex and variable due to environmental and physiological factors | Non-invasive; continuous sampling possible | Variability due to diet, environment, and metabolism |

Among the various types of wearable biosensors, skin-worn biosensor devices have garnered significant attention, primarily because the epidermis covers a substantial portion of the human body [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0029)]. These devices rely on collecting perspiration from the skin's surface or sampling interstitial fluid, facilitating the flow of biofluids across the biosensor surface. The widespread distribution of sweat glands across the body makes perspiration the most readily accessible biofluid for chemical sensing applications. As a result, these biosensor devices offer a promising avenue for efficient and non-invasive disease monitoring [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0030),[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0031)]. These sensors can be attached to the body through various means, including standard clothing, watches, mobile phones, and other everyday items integrated with sensors. Flexible sensors have emerged as a promising innovation, with advances in printed electronics and materials enabling tiny sensors to be used as skin patches. These biosensors are capable of measuring skin conductance, heart rate, body temperature, and detecting changes in pH [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0032),[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0033)], glucose [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0034)], and salt levels [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0016),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0035),[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666831925000888#bib0036)] within the human body. enriched_points.expanded_content: Wearable biosensors are portable devices that continuously monitor physiological parameters, revolutionizing healthcare by enabling real-time data collection and personalized health management.
These sensors facilitate ongoing health tracking, providing critical insights into biomarkers through non-invasive methods, ideal for chronic disease management and improving patient outcomes.
Real-time data empowers users by allowing immediate feedback on health metrics, fostering proactive health management and facilitating timely interventions before medical issues escalate.
Wearable biosensors integrate advanced sensor technology, including microfluidics and wireless communication, enabling seamless data transmission and enhancing the accuracy of health monitoring.
The adoption of wearable biosensors is transforming healthcare delivery by reducing hospital visits, enabling remote patient monitoring, and promoting preventive care through data-driven insights.
